title: Presenting music — performance
summary: This lesson provides an in-depth guide to the IB Music HL 'Presenting Music' component (performance). It deconstructs the assessment criteria, offers strategies for crafting a high-scoring programme, and provides actionable advice for developing technical proficiency and communicating deep stylistic understanding, moving beyond generic revision notes to target the top markbands.
learningObjectives: Understand and apply the IB assessment criteria for HL Music Performance (Criterion A: Technical Skills and Criterion B: Expression and Interpretation).
Develop strategies for selecting a balanced, challenging, and stylistically varied 15-minute programme.
Refine technical skills and expressive qualities through targeted practice and self-assessment.
Articulate a convincing and authentic stylistic understanding through informed musical interpretation.
Effectively communicate musical intentions to an audience, bridging the gap between technical execution and artistic communication.

simpleExplanation.title: Beyond the Notes: Mastering HL Performance
simpleExplanation.summary: Your HL Music performance is more than a technical test; it's a 15-minute musical argument. Examiners are looking for a performer who is not just an operator of their instrument, but a convincing musical storyteller who understands the language of the composers they present.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of yourself as a Shakespearean actor delivering a famous soliloquy. A novice might just recite the words correctly. A master actor, however, uses timing, intonation, gesture, and a deep understanding of the text's meaning and historical context to create a compelling, emotionally resonant performance. Your musical performance is your soliloquy; the score is your script, and your interpretation brings it to life.
steps: Select a balanced 15-minute programme that showcases a range of technical skills and stylistic periods, demonstrating your versatility.
Isolate and master the technical challenges in your pieces through methodical, slow practice before integrating them into the full musical context.
Research the historical context, performance practices, and stylistic conventions of each piece to inform your interpretative decisions on phrasing, articulation, and dynamics.
Record your practice sessions (audio and video) and critically analyse them as if you were the examiner, ensuring your musical intentions are clear and compelling.

flashcards.back: The effective combination of pieces in a recital that demonstrates a variety of styles, tempi, moods, and technical demands, showcasing the performer's versatility.

The ability to perform music in a manner that is authentic and appropriate to its historical period, genre, and cultural context. This includes conventions of articulation, ornamentation, and phrasing.
The accuracy of notes and rhythms, fluency, clarity of articulation, and, where applicable, intonation and tone quality. It is the foundational control of the instrument or voice.
The use of musical elements like dynamics, phrasing, rubato, and timbre to shape musical lines, convey emotion, and create a compelling interpretation.
The clarity with which the performer's musical ideas and interpretation are conveyed to the listener. A top-band performance has a clear and convincing artistic vision.
The smoothness and continuity of a performance, free from unintended hesitations or stumbles. It reflects a high level of preparation and confidence.
The accuracy of pitch, especially crucial for non-fixed pitch instruments (strings, wind, brass) and voice. It is a key component of technical proficiency.
The way in which a note is attacked, sustained, and released (e.g., legato, staccato, tenuto, accent). It is a primary tool for defining style and character.
The shaping of musical sentences. A well-phrased performance has a clear sense of direction, with notes grouped logically into gestures and lines that breathe.
The characteristic sound or 'colour' of an instrument or voice. A skilled performer can vary their timbre to suit different musical styles and expressive needs.
For group performances, this includes the ability to listen, blend, balance, and interact musically with other performers, maintaining rhythmic and harmonic cohesion.
For marks of 7-8, the performance demonstrates a 'consistently high level of technical control, fluency and accuracy'.
For marks of 7-8, the performance demonstrates a 'highly developed and convincing sense of style and musical expression'.

faq.q: Can I use a piece I am preparing for another examination, like ABRSM or Trinity?
faq.a: Yes, absolutely. You can and should use pieces from other exam syllabuses. However, you must ensure your overall 15-minute programme is balanced and meets the IB criteria for variety and challenge, not just the criteria for the other exam board.

faq.q: How much of my HL performance can be as part of an ensemble?
faq.a: For the 15-minute HL performance, you may include up to 10 minutes of music performed as part of an ensemble. In any ensemble piece, you must have a prominent, soloistic role that allows your individual technical and expressive skills to be clearly assessed.

faq.q: What happens if my performance is slightly over or under the 15-minute time limit?
faq.a: The IB allows for a reasonable degree of flexibility. A performance of 14 or 16 minutes is generally acceptable. However, a significantly short performance (e.g., 12 minutes) may not be considered substantial enough to demonstrate a sufficient range of skills and styles, potentially limiting your marks in Criterion B. Always aim for the 15-minute target.

faq.q: Do I need to submit the scores of the music I perform?
faq.a: Yes, you must submit clean copies of the scores for all the music you perform. The examiner uses these to assess your accuracy (Criterion A) and to see how your interpretation relates to the composer's intentions (Criterion B).

sections.content: The Presenting Music component at Higher Level is a substantial element of your final IB Music grade, accounting for 30%. It requires you to prepare and present a 15-minute solo performance (or a mix of solo and ensemble work). Success in this component hinges on more than just playing the right notes. It is an assessment of your holistic musicianship: your technical command, your intellectual engagement with musical styles, and your ability to communicate as an artist. This lesson will equip you with the strategies to excel in all these areas.

Your performance is assessed against two equally weighted criteria. Understanding these is the first step to a high-scoring presentation.

**Criterion A: Technical Skills (12 marks)**
This criterion assesses the fundamental 'correctness' and control in your playing. Examiners are listening for:
- **Accuracy:** Are the notes and rhythms correct as per the score?
- **Fluency:** Is the performance continuous and confident, without unintended stops or stumbles?
- **Clarity:** Is the articulation precise? Are musical lines distinct?
- **Control:** For your specific instrument/voice, this includes elements like intonation, tone quality, breath support, pedalling, or bowing technique.

**Criterion B: Expression and Interpretation (12 marks)**
This criterion evaluates your artistry and musical intelligence. It assesses how you go 'beyond the notes' to create a meaningful performance. Examiners are looking for:
- **Stylistic Understanding:** Does your performance reflect the conventions of the music's era and genre? (e.g., appropriate use of rubato in Romantic music, terraced dynamics in Baroque).
- **Expressive Qualities:** How effectively do you use dynamics, phrasing, articulation, and timbre to shape the music and convey mood?
- **Communication of Intent:** Is there a clear artistic vision? Does the performance feel purposeful and communicative?

items: Criterion A is the foundation: without technical security, expressive ideas cannot be realised.
Criterion B is the differentiator: this is where you demonstrate your musicianship and understanding.
A top-band performance shows a seamless integration of both criteria, where technique serves expression.

sections.content: Your choice of repertoire is your first, and perhaps most important, interpretative decision. A well-constructed 15-minute programme is not an arbitrary collection of pieces but a curated exhibition of your skills and musical knowledge. It should be challenging enough to allow you to demonstrate a high level of technical skill, but not so difficult that it compromises your fluency and control. The key is balance. Aim to include works that showcase a variety of moods, tempi, and, crucially, historical styles. This explicitly allows you to demonstrate the breadth of your stylistic understanding for Criterion B.

sections.question: A Grade 8 level cellist is selecting their 15-minute HL programme. They are confident with lyrical playing but need to demonstrate technical agility and stylistic breadth. Propose a balanced programme and justify the choices in relation to the IB assessment criteria.
sections.solution:
A strong programme for this cellist could be:

1.  **J.S. Bach - Prelude from Cello Suite No. 3 in C major, BWV 1009** (c. 4 mins): This demonstrates command of string crossing, resonance, and intonation in a contrapuntal texture. For Criterion B, it requires an understanding of Baroque phrasing, where harmonic rhythm guides the musical shape, and a dance-like character is maintained without romanticised vibrato or slides.

2.  **Camille Saint-Saëns - 'The Swan' from *The Carnival of the Animals*** (c. 3 mins): This plays to the cellist's strength in lyrical playing. It allows for a demonstration of beautiful tone production, controlled vibrato, and seamless legato phrasing. For Criterion B, it showcases a quintessential Romantic character, demanding a rich, expressive sound and subtle use of rubato.

3.  **Dmitri Shostakovich - First movement (Largo) from Cello Sonata in D minor, Op. 40** (c. 8 mins): This substantial movement provides dramatic contrast. It requires mature musicianship to sustain the slow, intense lines and build tension. It demonstrates technical control in the high register and the ability to handle complex harmonies. For Criterion B, it shows an understanding of 20th-century aesthetics—bleak, intense, and emotionally direct—contrasting sharply with the previous pieces.

**Justification:** This 15-minute programme is balanced. It moves from the structured elegance of the Baroque (Bach) to the lyrical expression of the Romantic era (Saint-Saëns) and concludes with the intense drama of the 20th century (Shostakovich). This variety provides ample opportunity to score highly in Criterion B by demonstrating convincing stylistic understanding across three distinct periods. The technical demands are also varied, covering bowing techniques, lyrical tone production, and navigating different registers of the instrument, allowing for a full demonstration of skills for Criterion A.

sections.content: Choose repertoire that you genuinely connect with and enjoy playing. Your engagement and passion for the music will translate into a more convincing and communicative performance. An examiner can tell when a performer is truly invested in the music.
From Practice Room to Performance: Bridging the Gap

Excellent performances are born from intelligent practice. Once your programme is set, your preparation should have two parallel streams: technical consolidation and interpretative development.

**Technical Consolidation:**
- **Isolate and Conquer:** Identify the 2-4 most technically demanding bars in each piece. Practise them slowly, in loops, with a metronome. Do not 'practise your mistakes' by always playing the piece from the beginning.
- **Mock Performances:** Regularly play through your entire 15-minute programme without stopping. This builds stamina and simulates the conditions of the final recording. It will reveal where your concentration lapses or where transitions between pieces are weak.

**Interpretative Development:**
- **Score Study:** Before solidifying your interpretation, study the score away from your instrument. Analyse the harmony, form, and texture. Mark key moments of tension and release. What is the composer trying to communicate?
- **Listen Widely:** Listen to several professional recordings of your pieces. Do not copy one interpretation, but analyse the choices different artists make regarding tempo, dynamics, and phrasing. Why did they make those choices? This will inform and broaden your own interpretative possibilities.

To earn top marks in Criterion B, your performance must be 'convincing' and 'idiomatic'. This means your playing choices align with the performance practices of the music's time. For example, playing a Mozart sonata with the wide, continuous vibrato and heavy rubato of a Tchaikovsky symphony would demonstrate a *lack* of stylistic understanding, no matter how technically well it was played. Your research into historical context is not just academic; it must be audible in your performance.

sections.question: Analyse how a singer would demonstrate 'stylistic understanding' (Criterion B) when performing Henry Purcell's aria 'When I am laid in earth' from *Dido and Aeneas*.
sections.solution: To deliver a stylistically convincing performance of this iconic Baroque aria, the singer must focus on specific period conventions. Firstly, the interpretation must be built around the text. Every word's meaning should be coloured by the tone, with clear, crisp diction being paramount. Vibrato should be used sparingly, primarily as a 'grace' or ornament on sustained notes to add emphasis, rather than as a constant, uniform oscillation typical of later operatic styles. The emotional weight is conveyed through the masterful use of the ground bass (a repeating bass line), and the singer must shape their phrases to align with its harmonic direction and inherent pathos. Ornaments, such as appoggiaturas or mordents, should be tastefully added, particularly at cadences, as was common practice, but they must enhance the melodic line, not obscure it. The overall delivery should possess a noble restraint, allowing the dissonances (like the one on 'death') and the chromatic descent of the ground bass to provide the dramatic impact, rather than imposing overwrought emotion. This synthesis of textual clarity, controlled vocal production, and harmonic awareness demonstrates a 'highly developed sense of style' appropriate for Purcell's music.
